```json
        "contents": "use mz_sql_parser::ident;\n\nuse crate::catalog::{CatalogItemType, CatalogType, SessionCatalog};\nuse crate::func::{self, Func, FuncSpec, TableFuncImpl};\nuse crate::names::{\n    Aug, FullItemName, PartialItemName, ResolvedDataType, ResolvedItemName, SchemaSpecifier,\n};\nuse crate::plan::PlanError::InvalidWmrRecursionLimit;\nuse crate::plan::error::PlanError;\nuse crate::plan::hir::{\n    AbstractColumnType, AbstractExpr, AggregateExpr, AggregateFunc, AggregateWindowExpr,\n    BinaryFunc, CoercibleScalarExpr, CoercibleScalarType, ColumnOrder, ColumnRef, Hir,\n    HirRelationExpr, HirScalarExpr, JoinKind, ScalarWindowExpr, ScalarWindowFunc, UnaryFunc,\n    ValueWindowExpr, ValueWindowFunc, VariadicFunc, WindowExpr, WindowExprType,\n};\nuse crate::plan::plan_utils::{self, GroupSizeHints, JoinSide};\nuse crate::plan::scope::{Scope, ScopeItem, ScopeUngroupedColumn};\nuse crate::plan::statement::{StatementContext, StatementDesc, show};\nuse crate::plan::typeconv::{self, CastContext, plan_hypothetical_cast};\nuse crate::plan::{\n    Params, PlanContext, QueryWhen, ShowCreatePlan, WebhookValidation, WebhookValidationSecret,\n    literal, transform_ast,\n};\nuse crate::session::vars::ENABLE_WITH_ORDINALITY_LEGACY_FALLBACK;\nuse crate::session::vars::{self, FeatureFlag};\nuse crate::{ORDINALITY_COL_NAME, normalize};\n\n#[derive(Debug)]\npub struct PlannedRootQuery&lt;E&gt; {\n    pub expr: E,\n    pub desc: RelationDesc,\n    pub finishing: RowSetFinishing&lt;HirScalarExpr, HirScalarExpr&gt;,\n    pub scope: Scope,\n}\n\n/// Plans a top-level query, returning the &#96;HirRelationExpr&#96; describing the query\n/// plan, the &#96;RelationDesc&#96; describing the shape of the result set, a\n/// &#96;RowSetFinishing&#96; describing post-processing that must occur before results\n/// are sent to the client, and the types of the parameters in the query, if any\n/// were present.\n///\n/// Note that the returned &#96;RelationDesc&#96; describes the expression after\n/// applying the returned &#96;RowSetFinishing&#96;.\n#[mz_ore::instrument(target = \"compiler\", level = \"trace\", name = \"ast_to_hir\")]\npub fn plan_root_query(\n    scx: &amp;StatementContext,\n    mut query: Query&lt;Aug&gt;,",

    {
      "codeBlock": {
        "relativeWorkspacePath": "./src/sql/src/plan/statement.rs",
        "range": {
          "startPosition": {
            "line": 279
          },
          "endPosition": {
            "line": 323,
            "column": 26
          }
        },
        "contents": "/// &#96;purify_statement&#96;. Feel free to rationalize this by thinking of those\n/// statements as not necessarily depending on external state.\n#[mz_ore::instrument(level = \"debug\")]\npub fn plan(\n    pcx: Option&lt;&amp;PlanContext&gt;,\n    catalog: &amp;dyn SessionCatalog,\n    stmt: Statement&lt;Aug&gt;,\n    params: &amp;Params,\n    resolved_ids: &amp;ResolvedIds,\n) -&gt; Result&lt;Plan, PlanError&gt; {\n    let param_types = params\n        // We need the &#96;expected_types&#96; here, not the &#96;actual_types&#96;! This is because\n        // &#96;expected_types&#96; is how the parameter expression (e.g. &#96;$1&#96;) looks \"from the outside\":\n        // &#96;bind_parameters&#96; will insert a cast from the actual type to the expected type.\n        .expected_types\n        .iter()\n        .enumerate()\n        .map(|(i, ty)| (i + 1, ty.clone()))\n        .collect();\n\n    let kind: StatementKind = (&amp;stmt).into();\n    let permitted_plans = Plan::generated_from(&amp;kind);\n\n    let scx = &amp;mut StatementContext {\n        pcx,\n        catalog,\n        param_types: RefCell::new(param_types),\n        ambiguous_columns: RefCell::new(false),\n    };\n\n    if resolved_ids\n        .items()\n        // Filter out items that may not have been created yet, such as sub-sources.\n        .filter_map(|id| catalog.try_get_item(id))\n        .any(|item| {\n            item.func().is_ok()\n                &amp;&amp; item.name().qualifiers.schema_spec\n                    == SchemaSpecifier::Id(catalog.get_mz_unsafe_schema_id())\n        })\n    {\n        scx.require_feature_flag(&amp;vars::UNSAFE_ENABLE_UNSAFE_FUNCTIONS)?;\n    }\n\n    let plan = match stmt {\n        // DDL statements.",

        "contents": "pub fn plan_params&lt;'a&gt;(\n    scx: &amp;'a StatementContext,\n    params: Vec&lt;Expr&lt;Aug&gt;&gt;,\n    desc: &amp;StatementDesc,\n) -&gt; Result&lt;Params, PlanError&gt; {\n    if params.len() != desc.param_types.len() {\n        sql_bail!(\n            \"expected {} params, got {}\",\n            desc.param_types.len(),\n            params.len()\n        );\n    }\n\n    let qcx = QueryContext::root(scx, QueryLifetime::OneShot);\n\n    let mut datums = Row::default();\n    let mut packer = datums.packer();\n    let mut actual_types = Vec::new();\n    let temp_storage = &amp;RowArena::new();\n    for (i, (mut expr, expected_ty)) in params.into_iter().zip_eq(&amp;desc.param_types).enumerate() {\n        transform_ast::transform(scx, &amp;mut expr)?;\n\n        let ecx = execute_expr_context(&amp;qcx);\n        let ex = plan_expr(&amp;ecx, &amp;expr)?.type_as_any(&amp;ecx)?;\n        let actual_ty = ecx.scalar_type(&amp;ex);\n        if plan_hypothetical_cast(&amp;ecx, *EXECUTE_CAST_CONTEXT, &amp;actual_ty, expected_ty).is_none() {\n            return Err(PlanError::WrongParameterType(\n                i + 1,\n                ecx.humanize_scalar_type(expected_ty, false),\n                ecx.humanize_scalar_type(&amp;actual_ty, false),\n            ));\n        }\n        let ex = ex.lower_uncorrelated(scx.catalog.system_vars())?;\n        let evaled = ex.eval(&amp;[], temp_storage)?;\n        packer.push(evaled);\n        actual_types.push(actual_ty);\n    }\n    Ok(Params {\n        datums,\n        execute_types: actual_types,\n        expected_types: desc.param_types.clone(),\n    })\n}\n\nstatic EXECUTE_CONTEXT_SCOPE: LazyLock&lt;Scope&gt; = LazyLock::new(Scope::empty);\nstatic EXECUTE_CONTEXT_REL_TYPE: LazyLock&lt;SqlRelationType&gt; = LazyLock::new(SqlRelationType::empty);\n\n/// Returns an &#96;ExprContext&#96; for the expressions in the parameters of an EXECUTE statement.\npub(crate) fn execute_expr_context&lt;'a&gt;(qcx: &amp;'a QueryContext&lt;'a&gt;) -&gt; ExprContext&lt;'a&gt; {\n    ExprContext {\n        qcx,\n        name: \"EXECUTE\",",

        },
        "contents": "(\n    qcx: &amp;QueryContext,\n    mut s: Select&lt;Aug&gt;,\n    mut order_by_exprs: Vec&lt;OrderByExpr&lt;Aug&gt;&gt;,\n) -&gt; Result&lt;SelectPlan, PlanError&gt; {\n    // TODO: Both &#96;s&#96; and &#96;order_by_exprs&#96; are not references because the\n    // AggregateTableFuncVisitor needs to be able to rewrite the expressions for\n    // table function support (the UUID mapping). Attempt to change this so callers\n    // don't need to clone the Select.\n\n    // Extract query options.\n    let select_option_extracted = SelectOptionExtracted::try_from(s.options.clone())?;\n    let group_size_hints = GroupSizeHints::try_from(select_option_extracted)?;\n\n    // Step 1. Handle FROM clause, including joins.\n    let (mut relation_expr, mut from_scope) =\n        s.from.iter().try_fold(plan_join_identity(), |l, twj| {\n            let (left, left_scope) = l;\n            plan_join(\n                qcx,\n                left,\n                left_scope,\n                &amp;Join {\n                    relation: TableFactor::NestedJoin {\n                        join: Box::new(twj.clone()),\n                        alias: None,\n                    },\n                    join_operator: JoinOperator::CrossJoin,\n                },\n            )\n        })?;\n\n    // Step 2. Handle WHERE clause.\n    if let Some(selection) = &amp;s.selection {\n        let ecx = &amp;ExprContext {\n            qcx,\n            name: \"WHERE clause\",\n            scope: &amp;from_scope,\n            relation_type: &amp;qcx.relation_type(&amp;relation_expr),\n            allow_aggregates: false,\n            allow_subqueries: true,\n            allow_parameters: true,\n            allow_windows: false,\n        };\n        let expr = plan_expr(ecx, selection)\n            .map_err(|e| sql_err!(\"WHERE clause error: {}\", e))?\n            .type_as(ecx, &amp;SqlScalarType::Bool)?;\n        relation_expr = relation_expr.filter(vec![expr]);\n    }\n\n    // Step 3. Gather aggregates and table functions.\n    // (But skip window aggregates.)\n    let (aggregates, table_funcs) = {\n        let mut visitor = AggregateTableFuncVisitor::new(qcx.scx);",

      "codeBlock": {

        },
        "contents": "fn plan_query_inner(qcx: &amp;mut QueryContext, q: &amp;Query&lt;Aug&gt;) -&gt; Result&lt;PlannedQuery, PlanError&gt; {\n    // Plan CTEs and introduce bindings to &#96;qcx.ctes&#96;. Returns shadowed bindings\n    // for the identifiers, so that they can be re-installed before returning.\n    let cte_bindings = plan_ctes(qcx, q)?;\n\n    let limit = match &amp;q.limit {\n        None =&gt; None,\n        Some(Limit {\n            quantity,\n            with_ties: false,\n        }) =&gt; {\n            let ecx = &amp;ExprContext {\n                qcx,\n                name: \"LIMIT\",\n                scope: &amp;Scope::empty(),\n                relation_type: &amp;SqlRelationType::empty(),\n                allow_aggregates: false,\n                allow_subqueries: true,\n                allow_parameters: true,\n                allow_windows: false,\n            };\n            let limit = plan_expr(ecx, quantity)?;\n            let limit = limit.cast_to(ecx, CastContext::Explicit, &amp;SqlScalarType::Int64)?;\n\n            let limit = if limit.is_constant()",
        "signatures": {},
        "detailedLines": [
          {
            "text": "fn plan_query_inner(qcx: &amp;mut QueryContext, q: &amp;Query&lt;Aug&gt;) -&gt; Result&lt;PlannedQuery, PlanError&gt; {",
            "lineNumber": 1456
          },
          {
            "text": "    // Plan CTEs and introduce bindings to &#96;qcx.ctes&#96;. Returns shadowed bindings",
            "lineNumber": 1457
          },
          {

        "contents": "#[enum_kind(PlanKind)]\npub enum Plan {\n    CreateConnection(CreateConnectionPlan),\n    CreateDatabase(CreateDatabasePlan),\n    CreateSchema(CreateSchemaPlan),\n    CreateRole(CreateRolePlan),\n    CreateCluster(CreateClusterPlan),\n    CreateClusterReplica(CreateClusterReplicaPlan),\n    CreateSource(CreateSourcePlan),\n    CreateSources(Vec&lt;CreateSourcePlanBundle&gt;),\n    CreateSecret(CreateSecretPlan),\n    CreateSink(CreateSinkPlan),\n    CreateTable(CreateTablePlan),\n    CreateView(CreateViewPlan),\n ShowCreate(ShowCreatePlan),\n    ShowColumns(ShowColumnsPlan),\n    ShowVariable(ShowVariablePlan),\n    InspectShard(InspectShardPlan),\n    SetVariable(SetVariablePlan),\n    ResetVariable(ResetVariablePlan),\n    SetTransaction(SetTransactionPlan),\n    StartTransaction(StartTransactionPlan),\n    CommitTransaction(CommitTransactionPlan),\n    AbortTransaction(AbortTransactionPlan),\n    Select(SelectPlan),\n    Subscribe(SubscribePlan),\n    CopyFrom(CopyFromPlan),\n    CopyTo(CopyToPlan),\n    ExplainPlan(ExplainPlanPlan),\n    ExplainPushdown(ExplainPushdownPlan),\n    ExplainTimestamp(ExplainTimestampPlan),\n    ExplainSinkSchema(ExplainSinkSchemaPlan),\n    Insert(InsertPlan),\n    AlterCluster(AlterClusterPlan),\n    AlterClusterSwap(AlterClusterSwapPlan),\n    AlterNoop(AlterNoopPlan),\n    AlterSetCluster(AlterSetClusterPlan),\n    AlterConnection(AlterConnectionPlan),\n    AlterSource(AlterSourcePlan),\n     {
      "codeBlock": {
        "relativeWorkspacePath": "./src/sql/src/plan/statement/dml.rs",

            "line": 294,
            "column": 28
          }
        },
        "contents": "    let limit = match finishing.limit {\n        None =&gt; None,\n        Some(mut limit) =&gt; {\n            limit.bind_parameters(scx, lifetime, params)?;\n            // TODO: Call &#96;try_into_literal_int64&#96; instead of &#96;as_literal&#96;.\n            let Some(limit) = limit.as_literal() else {\n                sql_bail!(\n                    \"Top-level LIMIT must be a constant expression, got {}\",\n                    limit\n                )\n            };\n            match limit {\n                Datum::Null =&gt; None,\n                Datum::Int64(v) if v &gt;= 0 =&gt; NonNeg::&lt;i64&gt;::try_from(v).ok(),\n                _ =&gt; {\n                    soft_panic_or_log!(\"Valid literal limit must be asserted in &#96;plan_select&#96;\");\n                    sql_bail!(\"LIMIT must be a non-negative INT or NULL\")\n                }\n            }\n        }\n    };\n    let offset = {\n        let mut offset = finishing.offset.clone();\n        offset.bind_parameters(scx, lifetime, params)?;\n        let offset = offset_into_value(offset.take())?;\n        offset\n            .try_into()\n            .expect(\"checked in offset_into_value that it is not negative\")\n    };\n\n    let plan = SelectPlan {\n        source: expr,\n        when,\n        finishing: RowSetFinishing {\n            limit,\n            offset,\n            project: finishing.project,\n            order_by: finishing.order_by,\n        },\n        copy_to,\n        select: Some(Box::new(select)),\n    };\n\n    Ok((plan, desc))\n}\n\npub fn describe_explain_plan",
        "signatures": {},
        "detailedLines": [
          {

    {
      "codeBlock": {
        "relativeWorkspacePath": "./src/sql/src/plan/query.rs",
        "range": {
          "startPosition": {
            "line": 179,
            "column": 80
          },
          "endPosition": {
            "line": 241,
            "column": 29
          }
        },
        "contents": "pub fn plan_ct_query(\n    qcx: &amp;mut QueryContext,\n    mut query: Query&lt;Aug&gt;,\n) -&gt; Result&lt;PlannedRootQuery&lt;HirRelationExpr&gt;, PlanError&gt; {\n    transform_ast::transform(qcx.scx, &amp;mut query)?;\n    let PlannedQuery {\n        mut expr,\n        scope,\n        order_by,\n        limit,\n        offset,\n        project,\n        group_size_hints,\n    } = plan_query(qcx, &amp;query)?;\n\n    let mut finishing = RowSetFinishing {\n        limit,\n        offset,\n        project,\n        order_by,\n    };\n\n    // Attempt to push the finishing's ordering past its projection. This allows\n    // data to be projected down on the workers rather than the coordinator. It\n    // also improves the optimizer's demand analysis, as the optimizer can only\n    // reason about demand information in &#96;expr&#96; (i.e., it can't see\n    // &#96;finishing.project&#96;).\n    try_push_projection_order_by(&amp;mut expr, &amp;mut finishing.project, &amp;mut finishing.order_by);\n\n    expr.finish_maintained(&amp;mut finishing, group_size_hints);\n\n    let typ = qcx.relation_type(&amp;expr);\n    let typ = SqlRelationType::new(\n        finishing\n            .project\n            .iter()\n            .map(|i| typ.column_types[*i].clone())\n            .collect(),\n    );\n    let desc = RelationDesc::new(typ, scope.column_names());\n\n    Ok(PlannedRootQuery {\n        expr,\n        desc,\n        finishing,\n        scope,\n    })\n}\n\n/// Attempts to push a projection through an order by.\n///\n/// The returned bool indicates whether the pushdown was successful or not.\n/// Successful pushdown requires that all the columns referenced in &#96;order_by&#96;\n/// are included in &#96;project&#96;.\n///\n/// When successful, &#96;expr&#96; is wrapped in a projection node, &#96;order_by&#96; is\n/// rewritten to account for the pushed-down projection, and &#96;project&#96; is\n/// replaced with the trivial projection. When unsuccessful, no changes are made\n/// to any of the inputs.\nfn try_push_projection_order_by(\n    expr: &amp;mut HirRelationExpr,\n    project: &amp;mut Vec&lt;usize&gt;,",
        "signatures": {},
        "detailedLines": [

    {
      "codeBlock": {
        "relativeWorkspacePath": "./src/sql/src/plan/query.rs",
        "range": {

          },
          "endPosition": {
            "line": 882,
            "column": 2
          }
        },
        "contents": "    if using.is_empty() {\n        if let Some(expr) = selection {\n            let ecx = &amp;ExprContext {\n                qcx: &amp;qcx,\n                name: \"WHERE clause\",\n                scope: &amp;scope,\n                relation_type: &amp;relation_type,\n                allow_aggregates: false,\n                allow_subqueries: true,\n                allow_parameters: true,\n                allow_windows: false,\n            };\n            let expr = plan_expr(ecx, &amp;expr)?.type_as(ecx, &amp;SqlScalarType::Bool)?;\n            get = get.filter(vec![expr]);\n        }\n    } else {\n        get = handle_mutation_using_clause(&amp;qcx, selection, using, get, scope.clone())?;\n    }\n\n    let mut sets = BTreeMap::new();\n    for Assignment { id, value } in assignments {\n        // Get the index and type of the column.\n        let name = normalize::column_name(id);\n        match desc.get_by_name(&amp;name) {\n            Some((idx, typ)) =&gt; {\n                let ecx = &amp;ExprContext {\n                    qcx: &amp;qcx,\n                    name: \"SET clause\",\n                    scope: &amp;scope,\n                    relation_type: &amp;relation_type,\n                    allow_aggregates: false,\n                    allow_subqueries: false,\n                    allow_parameters: true,\n                    allow_windows: false,\n                };\n                let expr = plan_expr(ecx, &amp;value)?.cast_to(\n                    ecx,\n                    CastContext::Assignment,\n                    &amp;typ.scalar_type,\n                )?;\n\n                if sets.insert(idx, expr).is_some() {\n                    sql_bail!(\"column {} set twice\", name)\n                }\n            }\n            None =&gt; sql_bail!(\"unknown column {}\", name),\n        };\n    }\n\n    let finishing = RowSetFinishing {\n        order_by: vec![],\n        limit: None,\n        offset: 0,\n        project: (0..desc.arity()).collect(),\n    };\n\n    Ok(ReadThenWritePlan {\n        id,\n        selection: get,\n        finishing,\n        assignments: sets,\n    })\n}\n\n//",

    {
      "codeBlock": {
        "relativeWorkspacePath": "./src/sql/src/plan.rs",
        "range": {
          "startPosition": {
            "line": 854,
            "column": 23
          },
          "endPosition": {
            "line": 922,
            "column": 50
          }
        },
        "contents": "pub struct SelectPlan {\n    /// The &#96;SELECT&#96; statement itself. Used for explain/notices, but not otherwise\n    /// load-bearing. Boxed to save stack space.\n    pub select: Option&lt;Box&lt;SelectStatement&lt;Aug&gt;&gt;&gt;,\n    /// The plan as a HIR.\n    pub source: HirRelationExpr,\n    /// At what time should this select happen?\n    pub when: QueryWhen,\n    /// Instructions how to form the result set.\n    pub finishing: RowSetFinishing,\n    /// For &#96;COPY TO STDOUT&#96;, the format to use.\n    pub copy_to: Option&lt;CopyFormat&gt;,\n}\n\nimpl SelectPlan {\n    pub fn immediate(rows: Vec&lt;Row&gt;, typ: SqlRelationType) -&gt; Self {\n        let arity = typ.arity();\n        SelectPlan {\n            select: None,\n            source: HirRelationExpr::Constant { rows, typ },\n            when: QueryWhen::Immediately,\n            finishing: RowSetFinishing::trivial(arity),\n            copy_to: None,\n        }\n    }\n}\n\n#[derive(Debug)]\npub enum SubscribeOutput {\n    Diffs,\n    WithinTimestampOrderBy {\n        /// We pretend that mz_diff is prepended to the normal columns, making it index 0\n        order_by: Vec&lt;ColumnOrder&gt;,\n    },\n    EnvelopeUpsert {\n        /// Order by with just keys\n        order_by_keys: Vec&lt;ColumnOrder&gt;,\n    },\n    EnvelopeDebezium {\n        /// Order by with just keys\n        order_by_keys: Vec&lt;ColumnOrder&gt;,\n    },\n}\n\n#[derive(Debug)]\npub struct SubscribePlan {\n    pub from: SubscribeFrom,\n    pub with_snapshot: bool,\n    pub when: QueryWhen,\n    pub up_to: Option&lt;Timestamp&gt;,\n    pub copy_to: Option&lt;CopyFormat&gt;,\n    pub emit_progress: bool,\n    pub output: SubscribeOutput,\n}\n\n#[derive(Debug, Clone)]\npub enum SubscribeFrom {\n    /// ID of the collection to subscribe to.\n    Id(GlobalId),\n    /// Query to subscribe to.\n    Query {\n        expr: MirRelationExpr,\n        desc: RelationDesc,\n    },\n}\n\nimpl SubscribeFrom {\n    pub fn depends_on(&amp;self) -&gt; BTreeSet&lt;GlobalId&gt;",
        "signatures": {},

        "contents": "// Copyright Materialize, Inc. and contributors. All rights reserved.\n//\n// Use of this software is governed by the Business Source License\n// included in the LICENSE file.\n//\n// As of the Change Date specified in that file, in accordance with\n// the Business Source License, use of this software will be governed\n// by the Apache License, Version 2.0.\n\n//! SQL planning.\n//!\n//! SQL planning is the process of taking the abstract syntax tree of a\n//! [&#96;Statement&#96;] and turning it into a [&#96;Plan&#96;] that the dataflow layer can\n//! execute.\n//!\n//! Statements must be purified before they can be planned. See the\n//! [&#96;pure&#96;](crate::pure) module for details.\n\n// Internal module layout.\n//\n// The entry point for planning is &#96;statement::handle_statement&#96;. That function\n// dispatches to a more specific &#96;handle&#96; function for the particular statement\n// type. For most statements, this &#96;handle&#96; function is uninteresting and short,\n// but anything involving a &#96;SELECT&#96; statement gets complicated. &#96;SELECT&#96;\n// queries wind through the functions in the &#96;query&#96; module, starting with\n// &#96;plan_root_query&#96; and fanning out based on the contents of the &#96;SELECT&#96;\n// statement.\n\nuse std::collections::{BTreeMap, BTreeSet};\nuse std::num::NonZeroUsize;\nuse std::str::FromStr;\nuse std::time::Duration;\n\nuse chrono::{DateTime, Utc};\nuse enum_kinds::EnumKind;\nuse ipnet::IpNet;\nuse maplit::btreeset;\nuse mz_adapter_types::compaction::CompactionWindow;\nuse mz_controller_types::{ClusterId, ReplicaId};\nuse mz_expr::{\n    CollectionPlan, ColumnOrder, MapFilterProject, MirRelationExpr, MirScalarExpr, RowSetFinishing,\n};\nuse mz_ore::now::{self, NOW_ZERO};\nuse mz_pgcopy::CopyFormatParams;\nuse mz_repr::adt::mz_acl_item::{AclMode, MzAclItem};\nuse mz_repr::explain::{ExplainConfig, ExplainFormat};\nuse mz_repr::network_policy_id::NetworkPolicyId;\nuse mz_repr::optimize::OptimizerFeatureOverrides;\nuse mz_repr::refresh_schedule::RefreshSchedule;\nuse mz_repr::role_id::RoleId;\nuse mz_repr::{\n    CatalogItemId, ColumnIndex, ColumnName, Diff, GlobalId,",

        "contents": "#[derive(Debug)]\npub struct ReassignOwnedPlan {\n    /// The roles whose owned objects are being reassigned.\n    pub old_roles: Vec&lt;RoleId&gt;,\n    /// The new owner of the objects.\n    pub new_role: RoleId,\n    /// All object IDs to reassign.\n    pub reassign_ids: Vec&lt;ObjectId&gt;,\n}\n\n#[derive(Debug)]\npub struct CommentPlan {\n    /// The object that this comment is associated with.\n    pub object_id: CommentObjectId,\n    /// A sub-component of the object that this comment is associated with, e.g. a column.\n    ///\n    /// TODO(parkmycar): &lt;https://github.com/MaterializeInc/database-issues/issues/6711&gt;.\n    pub sub_component: Option&lt;usize&gt;,\n    /// The comment itself. If &#96;None&#96; that indicates we should clear the existing comment.\n    pub comment: Option&lt;String&gt;,\n}\n\n#[derive(Clone, Debug)]\npub enum TableDataSource {\n    /// The table owns data created via INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE statements.\n    TableWrites { defaults: Vec&lt;Expr&lt;Aug&gt;&gt; },\n\n    /// The table receives its data from the identified &#96;DataSourceDesc&#96;.\n    /// This table type does not support INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE statements.\n    DataSource {\n        desc: DataSourceDesc,\n        timeline: Timeline,\n    },\n}\n\n#[derive(Clone, Debug)]\npub struct Table {\n    pub create_sql: String,\n    pub desc: VersionedRelationDesc,\n    pub temporary: bool,\n    pub compaction_window: Option&lt;CompactionWindow&gt;,\n    pub data_source: TableDataSource,\n}\n\n#[derive(Clone, Debug)]\npub struct Source {\n    pub create_sql: String,\n    pub data_source: DataSourceDesc,\n    pub desc: RelationDesc,\n    pub compaction_window: Option&lt;CompactionWindow&gt;,\n}\n\n#[derive(Debug, Clone)]\npub enum DataSourceDesc {\n    /// Receives data from an external system.",

        "contents": "                relation_expr = HirRelationExpr::top_k(\n                    relation_expr.map(map_exprs),\n                    distinct_key,\n                    order_by.iter().skip(distinct_len).cloned().collect(),\n                    Some(HirScalarExpr::literal(\n                        Datum::Int64(1),\n                        SqlScalarType::Int64,\n                    )),\n                    HirScalarExpr::literal(Datum::Int64(0), SqlScalarType::Int64),\n                    group_size_hints.distinct_on_input_group_size,\n                );\n            }\n        }\n\n        order_by\n    };\n\n    // Construct a clean scope to expose outwards, where all of the state that\n    // accumulated in the scope during planning of this SELECT is erased. The\n    // clean scope has at most one name for each column, and the names are not\n    // associated with any table.\n    let scope = Scope::from_source(None, projection.into_iter().map(|(_expr, name)| name));\n\n    Ok(SelectPlan {\n        expr: relation_expr,\n        scope,\n        order_by,\n        project: project_key,\n    })\n}\n\nfn plan_scalar_table_funcs",

        "contents": "//! in an &#96;ExprContext&#96; to find the precomputed versions.\n\nuse std::borrow::Cow;\nuse std::cell::RefCell;\nuse std::collections::{BTreeMap, BTreeSet};\nuse std::convert::{TryFrom, TryInto};\nuse std::num::NonZeroU64;\nuse std::rc::Rc;\nuse std::sync::{Arc, LazyLock};\nuse std::{iter, mem};\n\nuse itertools::Itertools;\nuse mz_expr::virtual_syntax::AlgExcept;\nuse mz_expr::{\n    Id, LetRecLimit, LocalId, MapFilterProject, MirScalarExpr, RowSetFinishing, TableFunc,\n    func as expr_func,\n};\nuse mz_ore::assert_none;\nuse mz_ore::collections::CollectionExt;\nuse mz_ore::error::ErrorExt;\nuse mz_ore::id_gen::IdGen;\nuse mz_ore::option::FallibleMapExt;\nuse mz_ore::stack::{CheckedRecursion, RecursionGuard};\nuse mz_ore::str::StrExt;\nuse mz_repr::adt::char::CharLength;\nuse mz_repr::adt::numeric::{NUMERIC_DATUM_MAX_PRECISION, NumericMaxScale};\nuse mz_repr::adt::timestamp::TimestampPrecision;\nuse mz_repr::adt::varchar::VarCharMaxLength;\nuse mz_repr::{\n    CatalogItemId, ColumnIndex, ColumnName, Datum, RelationDesc, RelationVersionSelector, Row,\n    RowArena, SqlColumnType, SqlRelationType, SqlScalarType, UNKNOWN_COLUMN_NAME, strconv,\n};\nuse mz_sql_parser::ast::display::AstDisplay;\nuse mz_sql_parser::ast::visit::Visit;\nuse mz_sql_parser::ast::visit_mut::{self, VisitMut};\nuse mz_sql_parser::ast::{\n    AsOf, Assignment, AstInfo, CreateWebhookSourceBody, CreateWebhookSourceCheck,\n    CreateWebhookSourceHeader, CreateWebhookSourceSecret, CteBlock, DeleteStatement, Distinct,\n    Expr, Function, FunctionArgs, HomogenizingFunction, Ident, InsertSource, IsExprConstruct, Join,\n    JoinConstraint, JoinOperator, Limit, MapEntry, MutRecBlock, MutRecBlockOption,\n    MutRecBlockOptionName, OrderByExpr, Query, Select, SelectItem, SelectOption, SelectOptionName,\n    SetExpr, SetOperator, ShowStatement, SubscriptPosition, TableAlias, TableFactor,\n    TableWithJoins, UnresolvedItemName, UpdateStatement, Value, Values, WindowFrame,\n    WindowFrameBound, WindowFrameUnits, WindowSpec, visit,\n};\nuse mz_sql_parser::ident;\n\nuse crate::catalog::",

        "contents": " {\n        &amp;self.recursion_guard\n    }\n}\n\nimpl&lt;'a&gt; QueryContext&lt;'a&gt; {\n    pub fn root(scx: &amp;'a StatementContext, lifetime: QueryLifetime) -&gt; QueryContext&lt;'a&gt; {\n        QueryContext {\n            scx,\n            lifetime,\n            outer_scopes: vec![],\n            outer_relation_types: vec![],\n            ctes: BTreeMap::new(),\n            name_manager: Rc::new(RefCell::new(NameManager::new())),\n            recursion_guard: RecursionGuard::with_limit(1024), // chosen arbitrarily\n        }\n    }\n\n    fn relation_type(&amp;self, expr: &amp;HirRelationExpr) -&gt; SqlRelationType {\n        expr.typ(&amp;self.outer_relation_types, &amp;self.scx.param_types.borrow())\n    }\n\n    /// Generate a new &#96;QueryContext&#96; appropriate to be used in subqueries of\n    /// &#96;self&#96;.\n    fn derived_context(&amp;self, scope: Scope, relation_type: SqlRelationType) -&gt; QueryContext&lt;'a&gt; {\n        let ctes = self.ctes.clone();\n        let outer_scopes = iter::once(scope).chain(self.outer_scopes.clone()).collect();\n        let outer_relation_types = iter::once(relation_type)\n            .chain(self.outer_relation_types.clone())\n            .collect();\n        // These shenanigans are simpler than adding &#96;&amp;mut NameManager&#96; arguments everywhere.\n        let name_manager = Rc::clone(&amp;self.name_manager);\n\n        QueryContext {\n            scx: self.scx,\n            lifetime: self.lifetime,\n            outer_scopes,\n            outer_relation_types,\n            ctes,\n            name_manager,\n            recursion_guard: self.recursion_guard.clone(),\n        }\n    }\n\n    /// Derives a &#96;QueryContext&#96; for a scope that contains no columns.\n    fn empty_derived_context(&amp;self) -&gt; QueryContext&lt;'a&gt; {\n        let scope = Scope::empty();\n        let ty = SqlRelationType::empty();\n        self.derived_context(scope, ty)\n    }\n\n    /// Resolves &#96;object&#96; to a table expr, i.e. creating a &#96;Get&#96; or inlining a\n    /// CTE.\n    pub fn resolve_table_name(\n        &amp;self,\n        object: ResolvedItemName,\n    ) -&gt;",

    {
      "codeBlock": {
        "relativeWorkspacePath": "./src/sql/src/plan/statement/dml.rs",
        "range": {
          "startPosition": {

          "endPosition": {
            "line": 1460,
            "column": 27
          }
        },
        "contents": "    let ctes = if !ctes.is_empty() {\n        format!(\n            \"WITH {}\",\n            separated(\n                \",\\n\",\n                ctes.iter()\n                    .map(|(name, defn)| format!(\"{name} AS ({defn})\"))\n            )\n        )\n    } else {\n        String::new()\n    };\n    let columns = separated(\", \", columns);\n    let from = separated(\" \", from);\n    let predicates = if !predicates.is_empty() {\n        format!(\"WHERE {}\", separated(\" AND \", predicates))\n    } else {\n        String::new()\n    };\n    // add mo.name last, to break ties only\n    order_by.push(\"mo.name DESC\");\n    let order_by = separated(\", \", order_by);\n    let query = format!(\n        r#\"{ctes}\nSELECT {columns}\nFROM {from}\n{predicates}\nORDER BY {order_by}\"#\n    );\n\n    if statement.as_sql {\n        let rows = vec![Row::pack_slice(&amp;[Datum::String(\n            &amp;mz_sql_pretty::pretty_str_simple(&amp;query, 80).map_err(|e| {\n                PlanError::Unstructured(format!(\"internal error parsing our own SQL: {e}\"))\n            })?,\n        )])];\n        let typ = SqlRelationType::new(vec![SqlScalarType::String.nullable(false)]);\n\n        Ok(Plan::Select(SelectPlan::immediate(rows, typ)))\n    } else {\n        let (show_select, _resolved_ids) = ShowSelect::new_from_bare_query(scx, query)?;\n        show_select.plan()\n    }\n}\n\npub fn plan_explain_timestamp(\n    scx: &amp;StatementContext,",

    {
      "codeBlock": {
        "relativeWorkspacePath": "./src/sql/src/plan/query.rs",
        "range": {

          }
        },
        "contents": " Vec::with_capacity(columns.len());\n\n    if columns.is_empty() {\n        // Columns in source query must be in order. Let's guess the full shape and truncate to the\n        // right size later after planning the source query\n        source_types.extend(desc.iter_types().map(|x| &amp;x.scalar_type));\n        ordering.extend(0..desc.arity());\n    } else {\n        let column_by_name: BTreeMap&lt;&amp;ColumnName, (usize, &amp;SqlColumnType)&gt; = desc\n            .iter()\n            .enumerate()\n            .map(|(idx, (name, typ))| (name, (idx, typ)))\n            .collect();\n\n        for c in &amp;columns {\n            if let Some((idx, typ)) = column_by_name.get(c) {\n                ordering.push(*idx);\n                source_types.push(&amp;typ.scalar_type);\n            } else {\n                sql_bail!(\n                    \"column {} of relation {} does not exist\",\n                    c.quoted(),\n                    table_name.full_name_str().quoted()\n                );\n            }\n        }\n        if let Some(dup) = columns.iter().duplicates().next() {\n            sql_bail!(\"column {} specified more than once\", dup.quoted());\n        }\n    };\n\n    // Plan the source.\n    let expr = match source {\n        InsertSource::Query(mut query) =&gt;",

        "contents": ";\n\n                if derived_typ.column_types.len() != proposed_typ.column_types.len() {\n                    return type_err(proposed_typ, derived_typ);\n                }\n\n                // Cast derived types to proposed types or error.\n                let val = match cast_relation(\n                    qcx,\n                    // Choose &#96;CastContext::Assignment&#96;&#96; because the user has\n                    // been explicit about the types they expect. Choosing\n                    // &#96;CastContext::Implicit&#96; is not \"strong\" enough to impose\n                    // typmods from proposed types onto values.\n                    CastContext::Assignment,\n                    val,\n                    proposed_typ.column_types.iter().map(|c| &amp;c.scalar_type),\n                ) {\n                    Ok(val) =&gt; val,\n                    Err(_) =&gt; return type_err(proposed_typ, derived_typ),\n                };\n\n                result.push((cte.id, val, shadowed_descs.remove(&amp;cte.id)));\n            }\n        }\n    }\n\n    Ok(result)\n}\n\npub fn plan_nested_query(\n    qcx: &amp;mut QueryContext,\n    q: &amp;Query&lt;Aug&gt;,\n) -&gt; Result&lt;(HirRelationExpr, Scope), PlanError&gt; {\n    let PlannedQuery {\n        mut expr,\n        scope,\n        order_by,\n        limit,\n        offset,\n        project,\n        group_size_hints,\n    } = qcx.checked_recur_mut(|qcx| plan_query(qcx, q))?;\n    if limit.is_some()\n        || !offset\n            .clone()\n            .try_into_literal_int64()\n            .is_ok_and(|offset| offset == 0)\n    {\n        expr = HirRelationExpr::top_k(\n            expr,\n            vec![],\n            order_by,\n            limit,\n            offset,\n            group_size_hints.limit_input_group_size,\n        );\n    }\n    Ok((expr.project(project), scope))\n}\n\nfn plan_set_expr(\n    qcx: &amp;mut QueryContext,\n    q: &amp;SetExpr&lt;Aug&gt;,\n) -&gt;",

        "contents": "fn plan_values(\n    qcx: &amp;QueryContext,\n    values: &amp;[Vec&lt;Expr&lt;Aug&gt;&gt;],\n) -&gt; Result&lt;(HirRelationExpr, Scope), PlanError&gt; {\n    assert!(!values.is_empty());\n\n    let ecx = &amp;ExprContext {\n        qcx,\n        name: \"VALUES\",\n        scope: &amp;Scope::empty(),\n        relation_type: &amp;SqlRelationType::empty(),\n        allow_aggregates: false,\n        allow_subqueries: true,\n        allow_parameters: true,\n        allow_windows: false,\n    };\n\n    let ncols = values[0].len();\n    let nrows = values.len();\n\n    // Arrange input expressions by columns, not rows, so that we can\n    // call &#96;coerce_homogeneous_exprs&#96; on each column.\n    let mut cols = vec![vec![]; ncols];\n    for row in values {\n        if row.len() != ncols {\n            sql_bail!(\n                \"VALUES expression has varying number of columns: {} vs {}\",\n                row.len(),\n                ncols\n            );\n        }\n        for (i, v) in row.iter().enumerate() {\n            cols[i].push(v);\n        }\n    }\n\n    // Plan each column.\n    let mut col_iters = Vec::with_capacity(ncols);\n    let mut col_types = Vec::with_capacity(ncols);\n    for col in &amp;cols {\n        let col = coerce_homogeneous_exprs(ecx, plan_exprs(ecx, col)?, None)?;\n        let mut col_type = ecx.column_type(&amp;col[0]);\n        for val in &amp;col[1..] {\n            col_type = col_type.union(&amp;ecx.column_type(val))?;\n        }\n        col_types.push(col_type);\n        col_iters.push(col.into_iter());\n    }\n\n    // Build constant relation.\n    let mut exprs = vec![];\n    for _ in 0..nrows {\n        for i in 0..ncols {\n            exprs.push(col_iters[i].next().unwrap());\n        }\n    }\n    let out = HirRelationExpr::CallTable {\n        func: TableFunc::Wrap {\n            width: ncols,\n            types: col_types,\n        },\n        exprs,\n    };\n\n    // Build column names.\n    let mut scope = Scope::empty();\n    for i in 0..ncols {\n        let name = format!(\"column{}\", i + 1);",

        "contents": "pub fn plan_as_of_or_up_to(\n    scx: &amp;StatementContext,\n    mut expr: Expr&lt;Aug&gt;,\n) -&gt; Result&lt;mz_repr::Timestamp, PlanError&gt; {\n    let scope = Scope::empty();\n    let desc = RelationDesc::empty();\n    // (Even for a SUBSCRIBE, we need QueryLifetime::OneShot, because the AS OF or UP TO is\n    // evaluated only once.)\n    let qcx = QueryContext::root(scx, QueryLifetime::OneShot);\n    transform_ast::transform(scx, &amp;mut expr)?;\n    let ecx = &amp;ExprContext {\n        qcx: &amp;qcx,\n        name: \"AS OF or UP TO\",\n        scope: &amp;scope,\n        relation_type: desc.typ(),\n        allow_aggregates: false,\n        allow_subqueries: false,\n        allow_parameters: false,\n        allow_windows: false,\n    };\n    let hir = plan_expr(ecx, &amp;expr)?.cast_to(\n        ecx,\n        CastContext::Assignment,\n        &amp;SqlScalarType::MzTimestamp,\n    )?;\n    if hir.contains_unmaterializable() {\n        bail_unsupported!(\"calling an unmaterializable function in AS OF or UP TO\");\n    }\n    // At this point, we definitely have a constant expression:\n    // - it can't contain any unmaterializable functions;\n    // - it can't refer to any columns.\n    // But the following can still fail due to a variety of reasons: most commonly, the cast can\n    // fail, but also a null might appear, or some other evaluation error can happen, e.g., a\n    // division by 0.\n    let timestamp = hir\n        .into_literal_mz_timestamp()\n        .ok_or_else(|| PlanError::InvalidAsOfUpTo)?;\n    Ok(timestamp)\n}\n\n/// Plans an expression in the AS position of a &#96;CREATE SECRET&#96;.\npub fn plan_secret_as(\n    scx: &amp;StatementContext,\n    mut expr: Expr&lt;Aug&gt;,\n) -&gt;",
```

</tool-use>

---


Here are focused pointers for enforcing “allowed range element type” at **planning** time.

---

## 1. **Main place: cast String → Range**  
**`src/sql/src/plan/typeconv.rs`** (lines **629–637**)

When planning `CAST(string AS range(...))` (or the equivalent via `CastStringToRange`), the target range type is `to_type` and the element type is `to_el_type`:

```629:637:src/sql/src/plan/typeconv.rs
            (String, Range) => Explicit: CastTemplate::new(|ecx, ccx, from_type, to_type| {
                let return_ty = to_type.clone();
                let to_el_type = to_type.unwrap_range_element_type();
                let cast_expr = plan_hypothetical_cast(ecx, ccx, from_type, to_el_type)?;
                Some(|e: HirScalarExpr| e.call_unary(UnaryFunc::CastStringToRange(func::CastStringToRange {
                    return_ty,
                    cast_expr: Box::new(cast_expr),
                })))
            }),
```

This is where the planner first commits to a concrete range element type for a string→range cast. Add a check here: if `to_el_type` is not an allowed range element type, return a `PlanError` (e.g. “range type over … is not supported”) instead of building the cast. That way invalid range types are rejected at plan time.

---

## 2. **Range type from the catalog**  
**`src/sql/src/plan/query.rs`** (lines **6149–6153**)

When a range type is loaded from the catalog (e.g. column type), `SqlScalarType::Range` is built from the catalog’s `element_reference`:

```6149:6153:src/sql/src/plan/query.rs
                CatalogType::Range {
                    element_reference: element_id,
                } => Ok(SqlScalarType::Range {
                    element_type: Box::new(scalar_type_from_catalog(catalog, *element_id, &[])?),
                }),
```

Today only built-in range types live in the catalog, so this path is “safe” for current types. If you ever support user-defined range types (e.g. `CREATE TYPE floatrange AS RANGE (subtype = float8)`), add the same “allowed element type” check here after `scalar_type_from_catalog` so invalid element types are rejected when the Range type is constructed from the catalog.

---

## 3. **Where range element type is used in operators**  
**`src/sql/src/func.rs`**

- **Lines 4811–4813** (`@>` with `RangeAny`, `AnyElement`): `elem_type` comes from the range-typed side and is passed into `BinaryFunc::RangeContainsElem`.
- **Lines 4845–4847** (`<@` with `AnyElement`, `RangeAny`): same idea for the range-typed side.

The concrete `elem_type` is determined by type resolution (e.g. unifying with the other operand); the actual cast is still planned in typeconv. So the **authoritative** place to reject invalid element types is (1) typeconv for casts and (2) query.rs for catalog types. You *could* add a defensive check in these operator branches as well, but it would be redundant if typeconv (and catalog path) are correct.

---

## 4. **Definition of “range element type”**  
**`src/repr/src/scalar.rs`** (around **3180**)

`unwrap_range_element_type()` is defined here on `SqlScalarType` (Range variant). The **set of allowed** range element types is not centralized in one constant; it’s implied by:

- Built-in range type names in **`src/sql/src/func.rs`** (e.g. `int4range` → Int32, `int8range` → Int64, `daterange` → Date, `numrange` → Numeric, `tsrange` / `tstzrange` → Timestamp/TimestampTz).
- The same set in **`src/catalog/src/builtin.rs`** (int4range, int8range, daterange, numrange, tsrange, tstzrange).

For a plan-time check, introduce a small helper (e.g. in `sql` or `plan/typeconv`) that takes `&SqlScalarType` and returns whether it’s an allowed range element type (Int32, Int64, Date, Numeric, Timestamp, TimestampTz), then call it from (1) and optionally (2).

---

## 5. **Summary**

| Location | Purpose |
|----------|--------|
| **`src/sql/src/plan/typeconv.rs`** (String → Range cast, ~629–637) | **Primary:** Reject invalid range element type when planning string-to-range casts. |
| **`src/sql/src/plan/query.rs`** (catalog Range, ~6149–6153) | **Optional/future:** Reject invalid element type when building Range from catalog (for custom range types). |
| **`src/sql/src/func.rs`** (e.g. 4811, 4846) | Uses `elem_type`; no need to check here if typeconv (and catalog) are validated. |
| **`src/repr/src/scalar.rs`** (~3180) | `unwrap_range_element_type()`; add “allowed” check in sql/plan, not here. |

So: add an “allowed range element type” check in **typeconv** when planning the String→Range cast, and optionally when constructing Range from the catalog in **query.rs**. That gives you plan-time errors instead of evaluation-time panics or runtime errors.

---

_**User**_

Ok, how do I return an error @src/sql/src/plan/typeconv.rs:630-638  here for a disallowed type. I don't see other errors?

And also, why don't I just restrict a  range to the allowed types?@src/repr/src/scalar.rs:1729-1731 

---

_**Agent (model default, mode Agent)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

The user has two questions:

1. **How to return an error in typeconv.rs** (lines 630-638) for a disallowed range element type — they don't see other errors in that context.

2. **Why not restrict the Range variant in repr/src/scalar.rs** so that `SqlScalarType::Range` only allows the allowed element types (e.g. via an enum or constrained type) instead of `Box<SqlScalarType>`?

Let me address both.

For (1): I need to look at how CastTemplate works and how other casts return errors (e.g. PlanError). The closure returns `Option<...>` or `Result<..., PlanError>`. Let me check the type of the CastTemplate closure return type and how plan_hypothetical_cast and similar code return errors.
</details></think>

---


Checking how the cast template returns errors and how `SqlScalarType::Range` is used.

The template signature is:
```rust
T: Fn(&ExprContext, CastContext, &SqlScalarType, &SqlScalarType) -> Option<C>
```
So the closure returns `Option<C>`. When we do `plan_hypothetical_cast(...)?`, the `?` on a `Result<T, E>` would require that we're in a function that returns Result. So the closure must return `Result<Option<C>, PlanError>` or something for `?` to work on PlanError. Let me check - in the (String, Array) case they have `plan_hypothetical_cast(ecx, ccx, from_type, to_el_type)?` - so the closure returns something that has `?` for Result. So the return type might be `Result<Option<Cast>, PlanError>`. Let me look at the CastTemplate::new again.

```rust
fn new<T, C>(t: T) -> CastTemplate
where
    T: Fn(&ExprContext, CastContext, &SqlScalarType, &SqlScalarType) -> Option<C>
```

So T returns Option<C>. So the closure t returns Option<C>. If inside t we use plan_hypothetical_cast(...)?, then ? would try to convert Err(e) into something. For Option, ? converts None to None. For Result, ? converts Err(e) to return Err(e). So the closure would need to return Result<Option<C>, PlanError> for the ? to work. So maybe the signature is different - let me look at the (String, Array) again.

```rust
(String, Array) => Explicit: CastTemplate::new(|ecx, ccx, from_type, to_type| {
    let return_ty = to_type.clone();
    let to_el_type = to_type.unwrap_array_element_type();

```

So plan_hypothetical_cast returns Result<..., PlanError>. So the closure must return Result<Option<Cast>, PlanError> for the ? to work. So the CastTemplate::new might have a different overload or the Fn trait might be different. Let me read the CastTemplate::new again.

Actually re-reading: `T: Fn(...) -> Option<C>`. So the return is Option<C>. If we have `?` on a Result, we need the closure to return Result<_, PlanError>. So there might be another impl or the type inference might be converting. Let me check - in Rust, if you return `Option<C>` and you have `foo()?` where foo returns Result<T, E>, then the ? would try to propagate the Err, but the function returns Option, so that would be a type error. So either (1) the closure returns Result<Option<C>, PlanError>, or (2) plan_hypothetical_cast returns Option. Let me check plan_hypothetical_cast.

---

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

So `plan_hypothetical_cast` returns `Option<mz_expr::MirScalarExpr>`, not Result! So the `?` in the closure converts `None` to `None` — i.e. if the hypothetical cast fails (returns None), the whole template returns None, and that means "no cast available". So we **cannot** return a PlanError from inside this CastTemplate closure; we can only return None to mean "this cast doesn't apply."

So to return an actual error (PlanError) for "disallowed range element type", we have two options:

1. **Change the cast lookup flow** so that when we're looking for a String->Range cast and we find a matching template, we run a separate check that can return PlanError. That would be in the code that *invokes* the cast template (the code that tries casts and collects errors). So we need to find where the cast is actually selected and applied — that's probably in a function that returns Result<..., PlanError> and that tries the template. If the template returns Some(cast), we might have a chance to validate the type there and return PlanError.

2. **Don't use the template for String->Range when element type is disallowed** — instead, have a separate code path that runs *before* or *alongside* the cast table. For example, when we're about to apply a String->Range cast, the code that does the application could check the range element type and return PlanError. So we need to find where the cast table is used and where we could add a check.

Let me find where CASTs are resolved and applied — where the typeconv code is called and how we could inject a PlanError for range.
</details></think>

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="read_file_v2">

</tool-use>

---

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

So the flow is:
1. `plan_cast(ecx, ccx, expr, to)` is called with `to = SqlScalarType::Range { element_type }`.
2. It calls `get_cast(ecx, ccx, from, to)` which looks up the cast table and invokes the CastTemplate. The template returns `Option<Cast>`. If it returns `Some(cast)`, we do `cast(expr)` and return Ok. If `None`, we return `Err(PlanError::InvalidCast { ... })`.
3. So the only way to get a *custom* error (e.g. "range type over float is not supported") instead of the generic InvalidCast is to either:
   - **Option A**: Make the String->Range template return `None` for disallowed element types (so we get InvalidCast with from=string, to=range(float)). That's not a great message.
   - **Option B**: Add an explicit check in `plan_cast` (or in the code path that calls cast_inner for String->non-String): when `to` is Range, before calling cast_inner, check if the range element type is allowed; if not, return Err(PlanError::...) with a custom variant or message.
   - **Option C**: Change the cast table machinery to support Result<Option<C>, PlanError> so the template can return Err. That would be a bigger change.

So the clean approach is **Option B**: in `plan_cast`, when we're about to do a cast to Range (either in the String->to branch or in the general cast_inner), check the target type. If it's Range with a disallowed element type, return a dedicated PlanError before ever calling get_cast. We need a helper like `validate_range_element_type(to: &SqlScalarType) -> Result<(), PlanError>` that returns Err with a nice message when the element type isn't one of the allowed ones.

Where exactly to put it: right at the start of plan_cast we could do:
  if let SqlScalarType::Range { element_type } = to {
      validate_range_element_type(element_type)?;
  }
That way any cast to a range type (whether from string or from something else) gets validated. Good.

Now for the user's second question: **Why not restrict Range to allowed types in repr/scalar.rs?**

Currently we have:
```rust
Range {
    element_type: Box<SqlScalarType>,
},
```

If we restricted it to an enum of allowed element types, we'd have something like:
```rust
Range {
    element_type: RangeElementType,  // enum { Int32, Int64, Date, Numeric(...), Timestamp(...), TimestampTz(...) }
},
```

Reasons NOT to do that (or why it's harder):
1. **SqlScalarType is in repr**, and repr is a lower-level crate. It's used everywhere—catalog, expr, sql, etc. So Range is used when we load types from the catalog (where we have type IDs that resolve to arbitrary scalar types), when we do type unification (RangeAny + AnyElement can produce Range(AnyElement)), and when we plan. So the "element type" often comes from the catalog or from type inference. If we made it an enum of only 6 variants, we'd have to map from SqlScalarType (Int32, Int64, Date, ...) into that enum when constructing Range, and we'd have to reject (at type construction time) any attempt to build Range(Float64). So we'd push the validation to the *construction* of SqlScalarType::Range. But who constructs it? The sql crate (query.rs from catalog, typeconv when we have to_type, func.rs when we have elem_type from unification). So we'd need every construction site to use a "safe" constructor that only accepts allowed types, or we'd have to have the enum and then a fallible conversion from SqlScalarType. So we could add something like `SqlScalarType::range(element_type: SqlScalarType) -> Result<SqlScalarType, PlanError>` in the sql crate that validates and then builds Range. But the repr crate wouldn't have PlanError. So we'd have to either (a) keep Range(element_type: Box<SqlScalarType>) in repr and validate in sql when we're about to use it for a cast/operator, or (b) introduce a new type in repr like RangeElementType that is an enum of the 6 allowed types, and then Range would hold that. But then we'd need to convert from catalog type IDs to RangeElementType—and the catalog can have user-defined range types in the future (e.g. floatrange). So we'd be forced to reject those at catalog load time. So (b) would mean Range in repr only has the 6 built-in element kinds; any other range type would have to be represented differently (e.g. we don't support it). That's a bigger design change.

2. **Simpler approach**: Keep `Range { element_type: Box<SqlScalarType> }` in repr (so we can represent "range over float" that came from the catalog or type inference), and add a **plan-time check** in the sql crate when we're about to use a range type for something that requires a supported element type (e.g. cast string to range, or when we build a range from catalog). That way we fail with a clear error at plan time without changing the type system.

I'll summarize for the user and show exactly where to add the check and how to return the error.
</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

So we have two options for the error:
1. Use existing `PlanError::Unsupported { feature: "range type over X".into(), discussion_no: None }` — message would be "range type over float not yet supported".
2. Add a new variant e.g. `UnsupportedRangeElementType { element_type_name: String }` with a clear Display.

I'll recommend adding a check in plan_cast and either a new variant or Unsupported. The user asked how to return an error — I'll show the concrete approach: add a helper that returns Result<(), PlanError> and call it at the start of plan_cast when to is Range. I'll use a new variant for clarity so the message can be "range type over {} is not supported" to match the runtime error.
</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

I have everything. Let me write the answer and the code.

1) How to return an error in typeconv: The CastTemplate closure only returns Option<C>; it can't return PlanError. So we don't return the error from inside the template. We add a check in `plan_cast` (which returns Result<HirScalarExpr, PlanError>) before calling cast_inner: when `to` is Range, validate the element type and return Err(...) if disallowed.

2) Why not restrict Range in repr: SqlScalarType is in repr and used everywhere; the element type often comes from the catalog or type inference (e.g. Range(UInt16) when the column is UINT2). Restricting to an enum in repr would require repr to know about "allowed" range types and would complicate catalog/inference. The cleaner approach is to keep Range(element_type: Box<SqlScalarType>) and validate at plan time in the sql crate.

I'll add:
- A helper in typeconv that checks if a SqlScalarType is an allowed range element type, and returns PlanError if not. We need a function that takes &SqlScalarType and returns Result<(), PlanError>. We could add a new PlanError variant UnsupportedRangeElementType(String) or use Unsupported { feature }.
- In plan_cast, right after `let from = ecx.scalar_type(&expr);`, add: if we're casting to Range, validate the element type.
- PlanError variant: I'll use a new variant so we can give a clear message like "range type over {} is not supported" (matching the runtime error). Let me add UnsupportedRangeElementType { element_type_name: String }.
</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

Now I need to add the `validate_range_element_type` function. It should take &SqlScalarType and return Result<(), PlanError>. Allowed: Int32, Int64, Date, Numeric { .. }, Timestamp { .. }, TimestampTz { .. }. I need to add it before plan_cast. I'll need to produce a human-readable name for the type in the error - the typeconv module has access to ExprContext for humanize_scalar_type, but the helper could just take the type and we need a way to get a string. Looking at PlanError::UnsupportedRangeElementType we have element_type_name: String. We could use a simple debug or a humanizer. The simplest is to use something like format!("{:?}", element_type) or we need to pass ecx into the validator to call humanize_scalar_type. Let me pass ecx so we can use ecx.humanize_scalar_type(element_type, false) for a nice message. So the signature becomes validate_range_element_type(ecx: &ExprContext, element_type: &SqlScalarType) -> Result<(), PlanError>. Then we need a way to check "is this an allowed range element type". We can match on element_type and return Ok(()) for the allowed variants, Err(...) otherwise.
</details></think>

---


Summary of what’s in place and why:

---

## 1. How to return an error in the cast template (630–638)

You can’t return a `PlanError` from inside that closure. The cast table uses **`CastTemplate`**, whose closure returns **`Option<Cast>`** (see around 84–92 in typeconv): `None` means “no cast,” and there’s no `Result`/`PlanError` in that signature.

So the error is raised **before** the template runs:

- In **`plan_cast`** (the function that returns `Result<HirScalarExpr, PlanError>`), we check the **target** type at the start.
- When the target is **Range**, we call a small validator and return **`Err(PlanError::UnsupportedRangeElementType { ... })`** when the element type isn’t allowed.
- The String→Range template is never run for invalid element types, so we never rely on it to produce an error.

So: “return an error here” is done in **`plan_cast`**, not inside the template at 630–638.

---

## 2. Why not restrict Range in `repr` to allowed types only?

Keeping **`Range { element_type: Box<SqlScalarType> }`** in `repr` is intentional:

- **`SqlScalarType`** lives in **`repr`** and is shared by catalog, type inference, and planning. The range element type often comes from:
  - the **catalog** (e.g. column type),
  - or **type inference** (e.g. `Range(UInt16)` when the other operand is `UINT2`).
- If you made `Range` hold only an enum of “allowed” element types in `repr`, you’d have to:
  - reject or map every other `SqlScalarType` at the boundary (catalog, inference), and
  - push “allowed range types” into `repr`, which is a lower-level crate and shouldn’t own SQL/catalog policy.
- So the type stays general in `repr`, and we **enforce** “allowed range element types” in the **sql/plan** layer when we actually use the type (e.g. when planning a cast). That keeps validation in one place and leaves `repr` generic.

---

## 3. What was implemented

1. **`PlanError::UnsupportedRangeElementType { element_type_name: String }`**  
   - New variant in `src/sql/src/plan/error.rs`.  
   - Display: `"range type over {} is not supported"`.

2. **`validate_range_element_type(ecx, element_type)`** in **`src/sql/src/plan/typeconv.rs`**  
   - Allows: **Int32, Int64, Date, Numeric, Timestamp, TimestampTz**.  
   - For anything else (e.g. Float32/64, UInt16/32/64), returns  
     `Err(PlanError::UnsupportedRangeElementType { element_type_name: ecx.humanize_scalar_type(...) })`.

3. **Use in `plan_cast`**  
   - At the start of `plan_cast`, if the target type is `SqlScalarType::Range { element_type }`, we call  
     `validate_range_element_type(ecx, element_type)?`.  
   - So any cast **to** a range type (including String→Range) fails at plan time with that error when the element type is disallowed.

With this, a query like  
`SELECT ('[1,100)')::int4range @> t5.c0 FROM t5`  
with a column type that forces a range over an unsupported element type will get a plan-time error:  
`range type over <type> is not supported`, instead of a panic or runtime error.

`cargo check` didn’t run successfully in your environment because of the rustup default toolchain; once that’s fixed, `cargo check -p mz_sql` should pass with these changes.

---

_**User**_

What about doing it in get_cast as opposed to plan_cast?

---

_**Agent (model default, mode Agent)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

The user is asking whether we could do the range element type validation in `get_cast` instead of in `plan_cast`.

Let me think about this:

1. **`get_cast`** returns `Option<Cast>` — i.e. it returns `Some(cast_fn)` or `None`. It doesn't return `Result<..., PlanError>`. So if we put the check in `get_cast`, we'd have to either:
   - Return `None` for disallowed range types (so the caller would get "no cast" and then `plan_cast` would turn that into `InvalidCast`), or
   - Change `get_cast`'s signature to return `Result<Option<Cast>, PlanError>` so we can return `Err(PlanError::UnsupportedRangeElementType)`.

2. If we just return `None` from `get_cast` when the cast is String->Range with bad element type, then `plan_cast` would hit the existing `None => Err(PlanError::InvalidCast { ... })` path. So the user would see "X does not support casting from string to range(...)" instead of "range type over float is not supported". So we'd lose the clearer error message unless we change how the error is produced.

3. If we change `get_cast` to return `Result<Option<Cast>, PlanError>`, then every caller of `get_cast` would need to handle the error. Let me check who calls `get_cast`.
</details></think>

---

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

So `get_cast` is only called from:
1. `plan_cast` (inside the `cast_inner` closure) — line 1281
2. `can_cast` — line 1320

`get_cast` returns `Option<Cast>`. If we put the validation inside `get_cast`:
- We could return `None` for String->Range with bad element type. Then `plan_cast` would produce `InvalidCast` ("does not support casting from string to range(...)"), not our custom message.
- To get the custom error we'd need `get_cast` to return `Result<Option<Cast>, PlanError>`. Then `plan_cast` would do `get_cast(...)?` and `can_cast` would need to handle the Result too — e.g. `get_cast(...).map(|o| o.is_some()).unwrap_or(false)` for can_cast, or we'd need a different approach for can_cast (e.g. a separate "can cast" check that doesn't return the custom error).

Actually the cleanest would be:
- In `get_cast`, when we're about to return a cast for (from, to) and `to` is Range, first check validate_range_element_type; if it fails, we need to return an error. So get_cast would need to return Result<Option<Cast>, PlanError>. Then:
  - plan_cast: let cast = get_cast(ecx, ccx, from, to)?; match cast { Some(c) => Ok(c(expr)), None => Err(InvalidCast...) }
  - can_cast: get_cast(ecx, ccx, from, to).map(|r| r.is_some()).unwrap_or(false) — but wait, if get_cast returns Err we want can_cast to return false (can't cast). So can_cast would be: get_cast(...).ok().and_then(|opt| opt).is_some() or matches!(get_cast(...), Ok(Some(_))).

So the change would be:
1. get_cast returns Result<Option<Cast>, PlanError>
2. At the start of get_cast (or right before we'd return Some(...) for a String->Range cast), when to is Range, call validate_range_element_type(ecx, to.unwrap_range_element_type())? 
3. plan_cast: get_cast(...)? then match Option
4. can_cast: get_cast(...).ok().flatten().is_some() — so "we can cast" only if get_cast returns Ok(Some(_)); if it returns Ok(None) or Err(_) we can't cast.

That way the validation lives in get_cast (one place for "when is a cast allowed") and we get the right error. The user asked "what about doing it in get_cast as opposed to plan_cast?" — so they want to move the check. I'll implement that.
</details></think>

---

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

So get_cast:
1. If from == to, return Some(identity)
2. Look up (from, to) in VALID_CASTS
3. If context allows, run the template; template returns Option<Cast>, so we return that

To put the check in get_cast we have two options:

**Option A: Check inside get_cast when `to` is Range, return None for bad element type**
- We'd return None, and plan_cast would then produce InvalidCast. So we lose the custom error message unless we also change how plan_cast handles None when the target was Range. That gets messy.

**Option B: Change get_cast to Result<Option<Cast>, PlanError>**
- When we have a potential cast to Range (i.e. we found a template and we're about to call it, and to is Range), first validate_range_element_type(ecx, to.unwrap_range_element_type())?; then proceed.
- Actually we should validate whenever we're considering casting TO a range type. So: after we get the template (or when we're about to return Some), if `to` is Range, validate. If validation fails, return Err(...). So get_cast needs to return Result<Option<Cast>, PlanError>.

Let me implement Option B:
1. get_cast returns Result<Option<Cast>, PlanError>
2. At the start of get_cast, after the from == to check, if `to` is Range, validate_range_element_type(ecx, to.unwrap_range_element_type())?;
3. Rest of get_cast unchanged but we wrap the final return in Ok(...)
4. plan_cast: cast_inner does get_cast(...)? then match on Option
5. can_cast: get_cast(...).ok().and_then(|o| o).is_some() or get_cast(...).map(|o| o.is_some()).unwrap_or(false)
</details></think>

Moving the check into `get_cast` so validation lives with cast lookup. Changing `get_cast` to return `Result<Option<Cast>, PlanError>` so we can return the custom error.

---


Summary of changes:

**Why `get_cast` works well**
- Validation lives next to “when is this cast allowed?” so any path that looks up a cast (including future callers) gets the same rule.
- `can_cast` automatically reflects it: disallowed range element types make `get_cast` return `Err`, and `can_cast` treats that as “cannot cast.”

**What changed**

1. **`get_cast`** now returns **`Result<Option<Cast>, PlanError>`**:
   - Right after the identity cast check, if `to` is `Range`, it calls **`validate_range_element_type(ecx, element_type)?`**. If that returns `Err`, `get_cast` returns that error.
   - The rest of the logic is unchanged; the final value is wrapped in `Ok(...)`.

2. **`plan_cast`**  
   - Removed the separate range check at the top.  
   - Uses **`get_cast(...)?`** so a range validation error from `get_cast` is propagated.

3. **`can_cast`**  
   - Uses **`get_cast(...).ok().and_then(|c| c).is_some()`** so:
     - `Ok(Some(cast))` → `true`
     - `Ok(None)` → `false`
     - `Err(...)` (e.g. unsupported range element type) → `false`

So the unsupported-range-element check is done only inside **`get_cast`**; **`plan_cast`** and **`can_cast`** both rely on it without extra logic.

```
[33mcommit 6b4d120560f2b8b6b2647e5fe84563ccf2ffa693[m
Author: mtabebe &lt;michael.abebe@materialize.com&gt;
Date:   Fri Feb 6 10:11:47 2026 -0500

    10006 [Planner] Add error in planning for unsupported range types
    
    Problem:
    Using an unsupported type with ranges (e.g., float) leads to a panic in
    either the optimizer or execution:
    &#96;&#96;&#96;
    ERROR: internal error: internal error in optimizer: internal transform error: unexpected panic during query optimization: internal error: entered unreachable code: UInt16(1) not yet supported in ranges
    &#96;&#96;&#96;
    
    Solution:
    Introduce a new error in get_cast that validates the range type
    (validate_range_element_type) to only the allowed range types (int32,
    int64, date, numeric, timestamp: https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/rangetypes.html)
    
    Introduce this in get_cast because this is used in the planning phase.
    If we simply introduce an error in the actual evaluation (e.g., in
    RangeBound::canonicalize) then it is possible that if the table is
    empty, an error is not thrown
    
    Testing:
    New tests in range.slt that tests ranges with uint2 and floats.

 3 files changed, 79 insertions(+), 6 deletions(-)

```

---


Summary of the review for **commit 6b4d120560**:

---

## PR review: 6b4d120560 — [Planner] Add error in planning for unsupported range types

### Verdict
**Approve** once the blocking item is fixed. The design (validation in `get_cast`, new error variant, slt tests) is good.

---

### Pre-merge checklist
- **One semantic change:** Yes — plan-time rejection of unsupported range element types only.
- **Testing:** New `range.slt` tests for uint2 and real (empty and non-empty tables).
- **Release notes:** Add a short note for this user-visible change (e.g. “Reject range types over unsupported element types at plan time with a clear error instead of an internal panic”).
- **Documentation:** Error and `get_cast` are documented; the doc bug below was the main issue.

---

### Blocking

**1. Wrong doc comment on `validate_range_element_type`**  
The doc for `validate_range_element_type` started with the “Similar to `plan_cast`…” paragraph, which describes **`plan_hypothetical_cast`** (the next function), not the validator.

**Fix applied:** The doc was replaced with a short description of what the function does (allowed range element types and that it returns an error otherwise). See the updated doc in `src/sql/src/plan/typeconv.rs`.

---

### Strong suggestions

**2. Trailing newline in test file**  
`test/sqllogictest/range.slt` did not end with a newline. A trailing newline was added so the file ends with a single newline.

**3. Run format/lint/clippy**  
Rust toolchain wasn’t available in this environment. Please run locally and fix any issues:

- `cargo fmt --check` (and `cargo fmt` if needed)
- `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets`
- `bin/lint`

---

### Nits
- Commit message could mention “timestamptz” in the list of allowed types.
- In `range.slt`, there are two blank lines before “# Test over tables with disallowed types”; one is enough.

---

### What looked good
- Validation in **`get_cast`** keeps cast-validity logic in one place and keeps `can_cast` correct.
- **`PlanError::UnsupportedRangeElementType`** and message (“range type over {} is not supported”) match project style.
- **Tests** cover empty and non-empty tables and both uint2 and real.

A full write-up is in `.cursor/pr-review-6b4d120560.md`. The blocking doc fix and the trailing newline in `range.slt` are already applied in your tree; you still need to run fmt/clippy/lint and add a release note.

---

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

The user wants to refactor the return type of `get_cast` from `Result<Option<Cast>, PlanError>` to a single level, using a new `CastError` enum that has:
1. A variant for "no cast available" (what was `None`) - e.g. `InvalidCast` or similar
2. Specialized variants like `UnsupportedRangeElementType` (what was `Err(PlanError::UnsupportedRangeElementType)`)

So the return would become `Result<Cast, CastError>` where:
- `Ok(cast)` = we have a cast
- `Err(CastError::InvalidCast { ... })` = no cast for this type combo (the old None case)
- `Err(CastError::UnsupportedRangeElementType { ... })` = we would have had a cast but the range element type is disallowed

I need to:
1. Define a `CastError` enum (probably in the same file or in plan/error.rs). The reviewer said "could be name simply InvalidCast" for the "no cast" variant - so maybe:
   - `CastError::InvalidCast` (or with fields for the existing InvalidCast message - name, ccx, from, to)
   - `CastError::UnsupportedRangeElementType { element_type_name }`

2. Change `get_cast` to return `Result<Cast, CastError>` instead of `Result<Option<Cast>, PlanError>`.

3. Update callers:
   - `plan_cast`: currently does `match get_cast(...)? { Some(cast) => Ok(cast(expr)), None => Err(PlanError::InvalidCast { ... }) }`. So we need to map CastError to PlanError when we're in plan_cast. So we need PlanError to be constructible from CastError, or we handle CastError in plan_cast and convert to PlanError (InvalidCast or UnsupportedRangeElementType).
   - `can_cast`: currently `get_cast(...).ok().and_then(|c| c).is_some()` - so it becomes `get_cast(...).is_ok()`.

For PlanError, we already have `InvalidCast` and `UnsupportedRangeElementType`. So CastError could be:
- CastError::InvalidCast { name, ccx, from, to } - when no cast exists
- CastError::UnsupportedRangeElementType { element_type_name }

And we need a way to convert CastError to PlanError. So impl From<CastError> for PlanError, or plan_cast does the match.

Actually re-reading the comment: "a new CastError enum, which would have one simple variant (e.g. could be name simply InvalidCast) that covers those things that have been None so far, and would also have more specialized variants".

So:
- CastError::InvalidCast - for the "no cast" case (maybe with name, ccx, from, to for the error message)
- CastError::UnsupportedRangeElementType { element_type_name }

Then get_cast returns Result<Cast, CastError>. So we never return Ok with "no cast" - we return Err(CastError::InvalidCast) when we would have returned Ok(None). But wait - when do we have "no cast"? When VALID_CASTS.get returns None, or when the template returns None. In those cases we don't have the cast, so we return Err(CastError::InvalidCast(...)). But we need the name, ccx, from, to for the error message - and get_cast doesn't have "name" (that comes from ecx.name in the caller). So we have ecx in get_cast. So we could have CastError::InvalidCast { ccx, from, to } and the Display impl or the conversion to PlanError could use ecx.name... but CastError would need to be constructed in get_cast, and we don't have the full PlanError context there. We have ecx, ccx, from, to. So CastError::InvalidCast could hold { ccx, from: String, to: String } and we'd need to add name when converting to PlanError. So:

CastError::InvalidCast { ccx, from, to }  // from and to are humanized strings
CastError::UnsupportedRangeElementType { element_type_name }

And in plan_cast when we do get_cast(ecx, ccx, from, to) we get Result<Cast, CastError>. On Err we convert to PlanError: for InvalidCast we add ecx.name and build PlanError::InvalidCast; for UnsupportedRangeElementType we build PlanError::UnsupportedRangeElementType. So we need From<CastError> for PlanError but we need ecx.name for InvalidCast. So we can't do a simple From. We need a method like CastError::into_plan_error(self, name: String) -> PlanError or we handle it in plan_cast with a match.

Let me define:
- CastError enum in typeconv.rs (or error.rs - the reviewer said "new CastError enum", and PlanError is in error.rs. So we could put CastError in error.rs and have PlanError hold or convert from it. Or keep CastError in typeconv.rs and have a conversion that takes ecx.name. I'll put CastError in typeconv.rs to keep the cast logic together, and plan_cast will convert to PlanError.)

Actually re-read: "Could we collapse this into one level somehow? Maybe by making a new CastError enum". So the enum is for the "error" case - the two kinds of failures. So Result<Cast, CastError> where CastError has InvalidCast (no cast) and UnsupportedRangeElementType. For InvalidCast we need to carry the data for the error message. So:

pub enum CastError {
    InvalidCast { ccx: CastContext, from: String, to: String },
    UnsupportedRangeElementType { element_type_name: String },
}

And get_cast returns Result<Cast, CastError>. When we would have returned Ok(None), we return Err(CastError::InvalidCast { ccx, from: ecx.humanize_scalar_type(from, false), to: ecx.humanize_scalar_type(to, false) }). When we would have returned Err(PlanError::UnsupportedRangeElementType), we return Err(CastError::UnsupportedRangeElementType { ... }).

Then plan_cast:
  let cast_inner = |from, to, expr| match get_cast(ecx, ccx, from, to) {
    Ok(cast) => Ok(cast(expr)),
    Err(e) => Err(e.into_plan_error(ecx.name.into())),  // or match and build PlanError
  };

So we need a method on CastError to convert to PlanError with the name. So:
impl CastError {
  pub fn into_plan_error(self, name: String) -> PlanError {
    match self {
      CastError::InvalidCast { ccx, from, to } => PlanError::InvalidCast { name, ccx, from, to },
      CastError::UnsupportedRangeElementType { element_type_name } => PlanError::UnsupportedRangeElementType { element_type_name },
    }
  }
}

That works. Let me implement.

Where to put CastError? In typeconv.rs we need it. If we put it in error.rs we'd have PlanError and CastError there, and typeconv would use CastError and convert to PlanError in plan_cast. Putting it in error.rs keeps all error types together. Putting it in typeconv.rs keeps cast-related types with cast code. I'll put it in typeconv.rs since it's cast-specific and the conversion to PlanError is a small method. We need to reference PlanError and CastContext from typeconv anyway.

Let me read the current get_cast and plan_cast and can_cast to get the exact code.
